infobox.key: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
infobox.value: (**UNC Chapel Hill**, **UNC**, **North Carolina** or **Carolina**)[[13]](#cite_note-13) is a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Chapel Hill, North Carolina](/wiki/Chapel_Hill,_North_Carolina "Chapel Hill, North Carolina"), United States. Chartered in 1789, the university began enrolling students in 1795, making it the [oldest public university in the United States](/wiki/Oldest_public_university_in_the_United_States "Oldest public university in the United States").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The **University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill** (**UNC Chapel Hill**, **UNC**, **North Carolina** or **Carolina**)[[13]](#cite_note-13) is a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Chapel Hill, North Carolina](/wiki/Chapel_Hill,_North_Carolina "Chapel Hill, North Carolina"), United States. Chartered in 1789, the university began enrolling students in 1795, making it the [oldest public university in the United States](/wiki/Oldest_public_university_in_the_United_States "Oldest public university in the United States").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The university offers degrees in over 240-270 distinct academic programs, divided among 13 professional schools and a primary unit, the College of Arts & Sciences.[[15]](#cite_note-15) It is [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") as "R1: Doctoral Universities – very high research activity" and is a member of the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities") (AAU).[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) The [National Science Foundation](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation") ranked UNC–Chapel Hill ninth among American universities for research and development expenditures in 2023 with $1.5 billion.[[18]](#cite_note-NCSES-18) Its [endowment](/wiki/Financial_endowment "Financial endowment") is $5.7 billion, making it the [ninth-wealthiest public academic institution in the United States](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_the_United_States_by_endowment "List of colleges and universities in the United States by endowment") as of 2024.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The campus covers 760 acres (310 ha), encompassing the [Morehead Planetarium](/wiki/Morehead_Planetarium "Morehead Planetarium") and the many stores and shops located on [Franklin Street](/wiki/Franklin_Street_(Chapel_Hill) "Franklin Street (Chapel Hill)"). Students can participate in over 550 officially recognized student organizations. UNC-Chapel Hill is a charter member of the [Atlantic Coast Conference](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_Conference "Atlantic Coast Conference") (ACC), which was founded on June 14, 1953. The university's athletic teams compete in 28 intercollegiate sports and are known as the [Tar Heels](/wiki/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels "North Carolina Tar Heels"). They have won 51 [NCAA](/wiki/NCAA "NCAA") team championships in eight different sports which ranks [eighth all time](/wiki/List_of_NCAA_schools_with_the_most_NCAA_Division_I_championships "List of NCAA schools with the most NCAA Division I championships"), and 52 individual national championships.

UNC-Chapel Hill is one of three corners of North Carolina's [Research Triangle](/wiki/Research_Triangle "Research Triangle"). The other two corners are [North Carolina State University](/wiki/North_Carolina_State_University "North Carolina State University") in [Raleigh](/wiki/Raleigh,_North_Carolina "Raleigh, North Carolina") and [Duke University](/wiki/Duke_University "Duke University") in [Durham](/wiki/Durham,_North_Carolina "Durham, North Carolina").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill](/wiki/History_of_the_University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill "History of the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/UNC-6-1819.pdf/page1-250px-UNC-6-1819.pdf.jpg)](/wiki/File:UNC-6-1819.pdf)

University of North Carolina course catalog from June 1819

The University of North Carolina was [chartered](/wiki/Charter "Charter") by the [North Carolina General Assembly](/wiki/North_Carolina_General_Assembly "North Carolina General Assembly") on December 11, 1789; its cornerstone was laid on October 12, 1793, at Chapel Hill, chosen because of its central location within the state.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) It is one of three universities that claims to be the [oldest public university in the United States](/wiki/Oldest_public_university_in_the_United_States "Oldest public university in the United States"), and the only such institution to confer degrees in the eighteenth century as a public institution.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

During the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), [North Carolina Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_North_Carolina "Governor of North Carolina") [David Lowry Swain](/wiki/David_Lowry_Swain "David Lowry Swain") persuaded [Confederate President](/wiki/President_of_the_Confederate_States_of_America "President of the Confederate States of America") [Jefferson Davis](/wiki/Jefferson_Davis "Jefferson Davis") to exempt some students from the draft, so the university was one of the few in the [Confederacy](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America") that managed to stay open.[[24]](#cite_note-24) However, [Chapel Hill](/wiki/Chapel_Hill,_North_Carolina "Chapel Hill, North Carolina") suffered the loss of more of its population during the war than any village in the South, and when student numbers did not recover, the university was forced to close during [Reconstruction](/wiki/Reconstruction_era_of_the_United_States "Reconstruction era of the United States") from December 1, 1870, until September 6, 1875.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Following the reopening, enrollment was slow to increase and university administrators offered free tuition for the sons of teachers and ministers, as well as loans for those who could not afford attendance.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Following the Civil War, the university began to modernize its programs and onboard faculty with prestigious degrees.[[27]](#cite_note-27) The creation of a new gymnasium, funding for a new chemistry laboratory, and organization of the Graduate Department were accomplishments touted by UNC president [Francis Venable](/wiki/Francis_Preston_Venable "Francis Preston Venable") at the 1905 "University Day" celebration.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Despite initial skepticism from university President [Frank Porter Graham](/wiki/Frank_Porter_Graham "Frank Porter Graham"), on March 27, 1931, legislation was passed to group the University of North Carolina with the [State College of Agriculture and Engineering](/wiki/North_Carolina_State_University "North Carolina State University") and [Woman's College of the University of North Carolina](/wiki/University_of_North_Carolina_at_Greensboro "University of North Carolina at Greensboro") to form the Consolidated University of North Carolina.[[29]](#cite_note-29) In 1963, the consolidated university was made fully [coeducational](/wiki/Coeducational "Coeducational"), although most women still attended Woman's College for their first two years, transferring to Chapel Hill as juniors, since freshmen were required to live on campus and there was only one women's residence hall. As a result, Woman's College was renamed the "[University of North Carolina at Greensboro](/wiki/University_of_North_Carolina_at_Greensboro "University of North Carolina at Greensboro")", and the University of North Carolina became the "University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill".[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), UNC was one of 131 colleges and universities nationally that took part in the [V-12 Navy College Training Program](/wiki/V-12_Navy_College_Training_Program "V-12 Navy College Training Program") which offered students a path to a Navy commission.[[33]](#cite_note-unorthcarolina-v-12-33)

In 1951, a court ordered the university to admit the first Black students to the schools of law and medicine. The first students were Harvey Beech, James Lassiter, J. Kenneth Lee, Floyd McKissick, and James Robert Walker in law and Oscar Diggs in medicine.[[34]](#cite_note-34) In 1955 after the U.S. Supreme Court outlawed all forms of segregation in the public schools, federal courts ordered the admission of black undergraduates to the university. The first three Leroy Frasier, John Lewis Brandon and Ralph Frasier were from Hillside High School in Durham, North Carolina.
Black enrollment remained low for many years. There were four black freshmen in 1960 and only eighteen in 1963.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

During the 1960s, the campus was the location of significant political protests. Prior to the passage of the [Civil Rights Act of 1964](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1964 "Civil Rights Act of 1964"), protests about local [racial segregation](/wiki/Racial_segregation "Racial segregation") which began quietly in [Franklin Street](/wiki/Franklin_Street_(Chapel_Hill) "Franklin Street (Chapel Hill)") restaurants led to mass demonstrations and disturbance.[[36]](#cite_note-36) The climate of civil unrest prompted the 1963 [Speaker Ban Law](/wiki/North_Carolina_Speaker_Ban "North Carolina Speaker Ban") prohibiting speeches by communists on state campuses in North Carolina.[[37]](#cite_note-37) This stand towards the racial segregation on campus led up to the [Sit-in movement](/wiki/Sit-in_movement "Sit-in movement"). The [Sit-in movement](/wiki/Sit-in_movement "Sit-in movement") started a new era in North Carolina, which challenged colleges across the south against racial segregation of public facilities. The law was immediately criticized by university Chancellor [William Brantley Aycock](/wiki/William_Brantley_Aycock "William Brantley Aycock") and university President [William Friday](/wiki/William_Friday "William Friday"), but was not reviewed by the North Carolina General Assembly until 1965.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Small amendments to allow "infrequent" visits failed to placate the student body, especially when the university's board of trustees overruled new Chancellor Paul Frederick Sharp's decision to allow speaking invitations to [Marxist](/wiki/Marxist "Marxist") speaker [Herbert Aptheker](/wiki/Herbert_Aptheker "Herbert Aptheker") and civil liberties activist [Frank Wilkinson](/wiki/Frank_Wilkinson "Frank Wilkinson"); however, the two speakers came to Chapel Hill anyway. Wilkinson spoke off campus, while more than 1,500 students viewed Aptheker's speech across a low campus wall at the edge of campus, christened "Dan Moore's Wall" by *[The Daily Tar Heel](/wiki/The_Daily_Tar_Heel "The Daily Tar Heel")* for Governor [Dan K. Moore](/wiki/Dan_K._Moore "Dan K. Moore").[[39]](#cite_note-39) A group of UNC-Chapel Hill students, led by Student Body President Paul Dickson, filed a lawsuit in [U.S. federal court](/wiki/United_States_federal_courts "United States federal courts"), and on February 20, 1968, the Speaker Ban Law was struck down.[[40]](#cite_note-40) In 1969, campus food workers of Lenoir Hall went on [strike](/wiki/UNC_Food_Worker_Strike "UNC Food Worker Strike") protesting perceived racial injustices that impacted their employment, garnering the support of student groups and members of the university and Chapel Hill community and leading to state troopers in riot gear being deployed on campus and the state national guard being held on standby in Durham.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

From the late 1990s and onward, UNC-Chapel Hill expanded rapidly with a 15% increase in total student population to more than 28,000 by 2007. This was accompanied by the construction of new facilities, funded in part by the "Carolina First" fundraising campaign and an [endowment](/wiki/Financial_endowment "Financial endowment") that increased fourfold to more than $2 billion within ten years.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43) Professor [Oliver Smithies](/wiki/Oliver_Smithies "Oliver Smithies") was awarded the [Nobel Prize in Medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Medicine") in 2007 for his work in genetics.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Additionally, Professor [Aziz Sancar](/wiki/Aziz_Sancar "Aziz Sancar") was awarded the [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry") in 2015 for his work in understanding the molecular repair mechanisms of [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA").[[45]](#cite_note-45)

In 2011, the first of several investigations found [fraud and academic dishonesty](/wiki/University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill_academics-athletics_scandal "University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill academics-athletics scandal") at the university related to its athletic program.[[46]](#cite_note-UNC_Scandal-46) Following a [lesser scandal that began in 2010](/wiki/University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill_football_scandal "University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill football scandal") involving academic fraud and improper benefits with the university's [football program](/wiki/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_football "North Carolina Tar Heels football"), two hundred questionable classes offered by the university's African and Afro-American Studies department came to light. As a result, the university was placed on probation by its accrediting agency in 2015.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) It was removed from probation in 2016.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

That same year, the [public universities](/wiki/Public_universities "Public universities") in North Carolina had to share a [budget cut](/wiki/Austerity "Austerity") of $414 million, of which the Chapel Hill campus lost more than $100 million in 2011.[[50]](#cite_note-The_Herald-Sun-Trusted&Essential-50) This followed state budget cuts that trimmed university spending by $231 million since 2007; Provost Bruce Carney said more than 130 faculty members have left UNC since 2009.,[[51]](#cite_note-WRAL.com-51) with poor staff retention.[[52]](#cite_note-52) The [Board of Trustees](/wiki/Board_of_directors "Board of directors") for UNC-CH recommended a 15.6 percent increase in [tuition](/wiki/College_tuition_in_the_United_States "College tuition in the United States"), a historically large increase.[[51]](#cite_note-WRAL.com-51) The budget cuts in 2011 greatly affected the university and set this increased tuition plan in motion[[50]](#cite_note-The_Herald-Sun-Trusted&Essential-50) and UNC students protested.[[53]](#cite_note-53) On February 10, 2012, the [UNC Board of Governors](/wiki/University_of_North_Carolina_Board_of_Governors "University of North Carolina Board of Governors") approved tuition and fee increases of 8.8 percent for in-state undergraduates across all 16 campuses.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

In June 2018, the [Department of Education](/wiki/Department_of_Education_(United_States) "Department of Education (United States)") found that the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill had violated [Title IX](/wiki/Title_IX "Title IX") in handling reports of [sexual assault](/wiki/Sexual_assault "Sexual assault"), five years after four students and an administrator filed complaints.[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) The university was also featured in *[The Hunting Ground](/wiki/The_Hunting_Ground "The Hunting Ground")*, a 2015 documentary about [sexual assault on college campuses](/wiki/Sexual_assault_on_college_campuses "Sexual assault on college campuses").[[57]](#cite_note-57) [Annie E. Clark](/wiki/Annie_E._Clark "Annie E. Clark") and [Andrea Pino](/wiki/Andrea_Pino "Andrea Pino"), two students featured in the film, helped to establish the survivor advocacy organization End Rape on Campus.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Silent_Sam.jpg/250px-Silent_Sam.jpg)](/wiki/File:Silent_Sam.jpg)

*[Silent Sam](/wiki/Silent_Sam "Silent Sam")*, a former campus statue

In August 2018, the university came to national attention after the toppling of [Silent Sam](/wiki/Silent_Sam "Silent Sam"), a [Confederate monument](/wiki/Confederate_States_Army "Confederate States Army") which had been erected on campus in 1913 by the [United Daughters of the Confederacy](/wiki/United_Daughters_of_the_Confederacy "United Daughters of the Confederacy").[[59]](#cite_note-Silent_Sam_Landmark-59) The statue had been dogged by controversy at various points since the 1960s, with critics claiming that the monument invokes memories of racism and slavery. Many critics cited the explicitly racist views espoused in the dedication speech that local industrialist and UNC Trustee [Julian Carr](/wiki/Julian_Carr_(industrialist) "Julian Carr (industrialist)") gave at the statue's unveiling on June 2, 1913, and the approval with which they had been met by the crowd at the dedication.[[60]](#cite_note-Green-60) Shortly before the beginning of the 2018–2019 school year, the Silent Sam was toppled by protestors and damaged, and has been absent from campus ever since.[[61]](#cite_note-61) In July 2020, the university's Carr Hall, which was named after [Julian Carr](/wiki/Julian_S._Carr "Julian S. Carr"), was renamed the "Student Affairs Building".[[62]](#cite_note-carrremoved-62) Carr had supported white supremacy and also the Ku Klux Klan.[[62]](#cite_note-carrremoved-62)

After reopening its campus in August 2020, UNC-Chapel Hill reported 135 new [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") cases and four infection clusters within a week of having started in-person classes for the Fall 2020 semester. On August 10, faculty and staff from several of UNC's constituent institutions filed a [complaint](/wiki/Complaint "Complaint") against its board of governors, asking the system to default to online-only instruction for the fall.[[63]](#cite_note-63) On August 17, UNC's management announced that the university would be moving all [undergraduate](/wiki/Undergraduate "Undergraduate") classes online from August 19, becoming the first university to send students home after having reopened.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

Notable [leaders of the university](/wiki/Leaders_of_the_University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill "Leaders of the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill") include the 26th Governor of North Carolina, [David Lowry Swain](/wiki/David_Lowry_Swain "David Lowry Swain") (president 1835–1868); and [Edwin Anderson Alderman](/wiki/Edwin_Alderman "Edwin Alderman") (1896–1900), who was also president of [Tulane University](/wiki/Tulane_University "Tulane University") and the [University of Virginia](/wiki/University_of_Virginia "University of Virginia").[[65]](#cite_note-65) On December 13, 2019, the UNC System Board of Governors unanimously voted to name [Kevin Guskiewicz](/wiki/Kevin_Guskiewicz "Kevin Guskiewicz") the university's 12th chancellor.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

In the early afternoon on August 28, 2023, the second week of the fall semester, a PhD student [shot and killed associate professor Zijie Yan](/wiki/Killing_of_Zijie_Yan "Killing of Zijie Yan") in Caudill Labs, a laboratory building near the center of campus.[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68)

In April 2024, UNC students joined [other campuses across the United States](/wiki/List_of_pro-Palestinian_protests_on_university_campuses_in_the_United_States_in_2024 "List of pro-Palestinian protests on university campuses in the United States in 2024")[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70) in protests and establishing encampments against the [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war") and the [genocide of Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinian_genocide_accusation "Palestinian genocide accusation") in [Gaza](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip"). Students demanded transparency in investments and that UNC [divest from Israel](/wiki/Boycott,_Divestment_and_Sanctions "Boycott, Divestment and Sanctions").[[71]](#cite_note-:2-71) With the administration coming down hard on the protesters,[[71]](#cite_note-:2-71) the students called for the protection of their [first amendment rights](/wiki/First_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "First Amendment to the United States Constitution"). 36 arrests were made with police clearing out the encampment that was set up in Polk Place.[[72]](#cite_note-72) Palestine Legal filed a federal civil rights complaint alleging that there was preferential treatment of Israeli students by UNC, and targeting of pro-Palestine students.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

## Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Campus")]

[![A brick building with a rusted dome and ionic columns.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Morehead_Planetarium.JPG/250px-Morehead_Planetarium.JPG)](/wiki/File:Morehead_Planetarium.JPG)

The [Morehead Planetarium](/wiki/Morehead_Planetarium "Morehead Planetarium"), designed by [Eggers & Higgins](/wiki/Eggers_%26_Higgins "Eggers & Higgins"), first opened in 1949.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/UNC_Health_Care.JPG/250px-UNC_Health_Care.JPG)](/wiki/File:UNC_Health_Care.JPG)

[UNC Medical Center](/wiki/UNC_Medical_Center "UNC Medical Center")

UNC-Chapel Hill's campus covers around 760 acres (310 ha), including about 125 acres (51 ha) of lawns and over 30 acres (12 ha) of shrub beds and other ground cover.[[75]](#cite_note-75) In 1999, UNC-Chapel Hill was one of sixteen recipients of the [American Society of Landscape Architects](/wiki/American_Society_of_Landscape_Architects "American Society of Landscape Architects") Medallion Awards and was identified (in the second tier) as one of 50 college or university "works of art" by T.A. Gaines in his book *The Campus as a Work of Art*.[[76]](#cite_note-beautiful-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)

[![A seal for a school that contains the name and the date of founding.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/UNC_Seal.JPG/250px-UNC_Seal.JPG)](/wiki/File:UNC_Seal.JPG)

A representation of the university seal, located in front of South Building

The oldest buildings on the campus, including the Old East building (built 1793–1795),[[78]](#cite_note-OldSouth-78) the South Building (built 1798–1814),[[79]](#cite_note-79) and the Old West building (built 1822–1823),[[80]](#cite_note-80) stand around a [quadrangle](/wiki/Quadrangle_(architecture) "Quadrangle (architecture)") that runs north to Chapel Hill.[[78]](#cite_note-OldSouth-78) This is named McCorkle Place after [Samuel Eusebius McCorkle](/wiki/Samuel_Eusebius_McCorkle "Samuel Eusebius McCorkle"), who campaigned for the foundation of the university and was the original author of the [bill](/wiki/Bill_(proposed_law) "Bill (proposed law)") requesting the university's [charter](/wiki/Charter "Charter").[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82)

A second quadrangle, Polk Place, was built in the 1920s to the south of the original campus, with the South Building on its north side, and named after North Carolina native and university alumnus President [James K. Polk](/wiki/James_K._Polk "James K. Polk"). The [Wilson Library](/wiki/Louis_Round_Wilson_Library "Louis Round Wilson Library") is at the south end of Polk Place.[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84)

McCorkle Place and Polk Place are both in what is the northern part of the campus in the 21st century, along with
the Frank Porter Graham Student Union, and the Davis, House, and Wilson libraries. Most university classrooms are located in this area, along with several undergraduate residence halls.[[85]](#cite_note-campusmap-85) The middle part of the campus includes [Fetzer Field](/wiki/Fetzer_Field "Fetzer Field") and [Woollen Gymnasium](/wiki/Woollen_Gymnasium "Woollen Gymnasium") along with the Student Recreation Center, [Kenan Memorial Stadium](/wiki/Kenan_Memorial_Stadium "Kenan Memorial Stadium"), Irwin Belk outdoor track, [Eddie Smith Field House](/wiki/Eddie_Smith_Field_House "Eddie Smith Field House"), [Boshamer Stadium](/wiki/Boshamer_Stadium "Boshamer Stadium"), [Carmichael Auditorium](/wiki/Carmichael_Auditorium "Carmichael Auditorium"), [Sonja Haynes Stone Center for Black Culture and History](/wiki/Sonja_Haynes_Stone_Center_for_Black_Culture_and_History "Sonja Haynes Stone Center for Black Culture and History"), School of Government, [School of Law](/wiki/University_of_North_Carolina_School_of_Law "University of North Carolina School of Law"), George Watts Hill Alumni Center, Ram's Head complex (with a dining hall, parking garage, grocery store, and gymnasium), and various residence halls.[[85]](#cite_note-campusmap-85) The southern part of the campus houses the [Dean Smith Center](/wiki/Dean_Smith_Center "Dean Smith Center") for men's basketball, Koury Natatorium, [School of Medicine](/wiki/University_of_North_Carolina_School_of_Medicine "University of North Carolina School of Medicine"), [Adams School of Dentistry](/wiki/UNC_Adams_School_of_Dentistry "UNC Adams School of Dentistry"), [Eshelman School of Pharmacy](/wiki/UNC_Eshelman_School_of_Pharmacy "UNC Eshelman School of Pharmacy"), [Gillings School of Global Public Health](/wiki/UNC_Gillings_School_of_Global_Public_Health "UNC Gillings School of Global Public Health"), [UNC Hospitals](/wiki/UNC_Health_Care "UNC Health Care"), [Kenan–Flagler Business School](/wiki/Kenan%E2%80%93Flagler_Business_School "Kenan–Flagler Business School"), and the newest student residence halls.[[85]](#cite_note-campusmap-85)

### Campus features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Campus features")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Old_Well_UNC.jpg/250px-Old_Well_UNC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_Well_UNC.jpg)

Students walk past the [Old Well](/wiki/Old_Well "Old Well"), a symbol of UNC-Chapel Hill for years

[![A bell tower in the night with lit up archways at the base and a clock near the top of the tower.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/The_Morehead_Patterson_Bell_Tower.jpg/250px-The_Morehead_Patterson_Bell_Tower.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Morehead_Patterson_Bell_Tower.jpg)

The Morehead–Patterson Bell Tower was completed in 1931 and stands 172 feet tall.[[86]](#cite_note-unc.edu-86)

Located in McCorkle Place is the [Davie Poplar](/wiki/Davie_Poplar "Davie Poplar") tree under which a popular legend says the university's founder, [William Richardson Davie](/wiki/William_Richardson_Davie "William Richardson Davie"), selected the location for the university. The legend of the Davie Poplar says that as long as the tree stands, so will the university.[[87]](#cite_note-poplar-87) However, the name was not associated with the tree until almost a century after the university's foundation.[[88]](#cite_note-McCorklePlace-88) A graft from the tree, named Davie Poplar Jr., was planted nearby in 1918 after the original tree was struck by lightning.[[88]](#cite_note-McCorklePlace-88) A second graft, Davie Poplar III, was planted in conjunction with the university's [bicentennial](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/bicentennial "wikt:bicentennial") celebration in 1993.[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-:1-90) The student members of the university's [Dialectic and Philanthropic Societies](/wiki/Dialectic_and_Philanthropic_Societies "Dialectic and Philanthropic Societies") are not allowed to walk on the grass of McCorkle Place out of respect for the unknown resting place of [Joseph Caldwell](/wiki/Joseph_Caldwell "Joseph Caldwell"), the university's first president.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

A symbol of the university is the [Old Well](/wiki/Old_Well "Old Well"), a small neoclassical [rotunda](/wiki/Rotunda_(architecture) "Rotunda (architecture)") at the south end of McCorkle Place based on the Temple of Love in the [Gardens of Versailles](/wiki/Gardens_of_Versailles "Gardens of Versailles"), in the same location as the original well that provided water for the school.[[92]](#cite_note-landmarks-92) The well stands at the south end of McCorkle Place, the northern quad, between two of the campus's oldest buildings, [Old East](/wiki/Old_East "Old East"), and Old West.

The historic [Playmakers Theatre](/wiki/Playmakers_Theatre "Playmakers Theatre") is located on Cameron Avenue between McCorkle Place and Polk Place. It was designed by [Alexander Jackson Davis](/wiki/Alexander_Jackson_Davis "Alexander Jackson Davis"), the same architect who renovated the northern façade of [Old East](/wiki/Old_East "Old East") in 1844.[[93]](#cite_note-93) The east-facing building was completed in 1851 and initially served as a library and as a ballroom. It was originally named Smith Hall after North Carolina Governor [General Benjamin Smith](/wiki/Benjamin_Smith_(North_Carolina) "Benjamin Smith (North Carolina)"), who was a special aide to [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") during the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") and was an early benefactor to the university.[[94]](#cite_note-94) When the library moved to Hill Hall in 1907, the building was transferred between the school of law and the agricultural chemistry department until it was taken over by the university theater group, the Carolina Playmakers, in 1924. It was remodeled as a theater, opening in 1925 as Playmakers Theater.[[95]](#cite_note-95) Playmakers Theatre was declared a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") in 1973.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

The Morehead–Patterson bell tower, south of the Wilson Library, was commissioned by [John Motley Morehead III](/wiki/John_Motley_Morehead_III "John Motley Morehead III"), the benefactor of the [Morehead-Cain Scholarship](/wiki/Morehead-Cain_Scholarship "Morehead-Cain Scholarship").[[97]](#cite_note-97) The hedge and surrounding landscape was designed by [William C. Coker](/wiki/William_Chambers_Coker "William Chambers Coker"), botany professor and creator of the [campus arboretum](/wiki/Coker_Arboretum "Coker Arboretum"). Traditionally, seniors have the opportunity to climb the tower a few days prior to May commencement.[[86]](#cite_note-unc.edu-86)

### Environment and sustainability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Environment and sustainability")]

The university has a goal that all new buildings meet the requirements for [LEED](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design "Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design") silver certification,[[98]](#cite_note-UNC_Sustainability:_Buildings-98) and the Allen Education Center at the university's [North Carolina Botanic Garden](/wiki/North_Carolina_Botanical_Garden "North Carolina Botanical Garden") was the first building in North Carolina to receive LEED Platinum certification.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

UNC-Chapel Hill's [cogeneration](/wiki/Cogeneration "Cogeneration") facility produced one-fourth of the electricity and all of the steam used on campus as of 2008.[[100]](#cite_note-UNC_Sustainability:_Energy_at_UNC-100)
In 2006, the university and the [Town of Chapel Hill](/wiki/Chapel_Hill,_North_Carolina "Chapel Hill, North Carolina") jointly agreed to reduce greenhouse gas emissions 60% by 2050, becoming the first town-gown partnership in the country to make such an agreement.[[101]](#cite_note-UNC_Sustainability:_Institutionalizing_Sustainability-101)
Through these efforts, the university achieved an "A−" grade on the Sustainable Endowment Institute's College Sustainability Report Card 2010.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

The university was criticized in 2019 for abandoning a promise to shutter its coal-fired power plant by 2020.[[103]](#cite_note-103) Initially, the university has announced plans to become carbon neutral by 2050, but in 2021, the plan was changed to 2040.[[104]](#cite_note-104) In December 2019, the university was sued by the [Sierra Club](/wiki/Sierra_Club "Sierra Club") and the [Center for Biological Diversity](/wiki/Center_for_Biological_Diversity "Center for Biological Diversity") for violations of the [Clean Air Act](/wiki/Clean_Air_Act_(United_States) "Clean Air Act (United States)").[[105]](#cite_note-105)

### Curriculum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Curriculum")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Students_walking_through_UNC_campus.jpg/250px-Students_walking_through_UNC_campus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Students_walking_through_UNC_campus.jpg)

Students on campus between classes

As of 2007,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit) UNC-Chapel Hill offered 71 bachelor's, 107 master's and 74 [doctoral degree](/wiki/Doctorate "Doctorate") programs.[[122]](#cite_note-compendium-122) The university enrolls students from all 100 [North Carolina counties](/wiki/List_of_counties_in_North_Carolina "List of counties in North Carolina") and state law requires that the percentage of students from North Carolina in each [freshman](/wiki/Freshman "Freshman") class meet or exceed 82%.[[123]](#cite_note-123) The student body consists of 17,981 undergraduate students and 10,935 graduate and professional students (as of Fall 2009).[[124]](#cite_note-124) Racial and ethnic minorities comprise 30.8% of UNC-Chapel Hill's undergraduate population as of 2010[[125]](#cite_note-125) and applications from international students more than doubled in five years from 702 in 2004 to 1,629 in 2009.[[126]](#cite_note-126) Eighty-nine percent of enrolling first year students in 2009 reported a GPA of 4.0 or higher on a weighted 4.0 scale.[[127]](#cite_note-admissions.unc.edu-127) The most popular majors at UNC-Chapel Hill in 2009 were biology, business administration, psychology, media and journalism, and political science.[[127]](#cite_note-admissions.unc.edu-127) UNC-Chapel Hill also offers 300 study abroad programs in 70 countries.[[128]](#cite_note-128)

At the undergraduate level, all students must fulfill a number of general education requirements as part of the Making Connections curriculum, which was introduced in 2006.[[129]](#cite_note-129) English, social science, history, foreign language, mathematics, and natural science courses are required of all students, ensuring that they receive a broad [liberal arts](/wiki/Liberal_arts "Liberal arts") education.[[130]](#cite_note-130) The university also offers a wide range of first year seminars for incoming freshmen.[[131]](#cite_note-131) After their second year, students move on to the College of Arts and Sciences, or choose an undergraduate professional school program within the schools of medicine, nursing, business, education, pharmacy, information and library science, public health, or media and journalism.[[132]](#cite_note-132) Undergraduates are held to an eight-semester limit of study.[[133]](#cite_note-133)

UNC-Chapel Hill's admissions process is "most selective" according to *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")*.[[136]](#cite_note-136) For the Class of 2025 (enrolled fall 2021), UNC-Chapel Hill received 53,776 applications and accepted 10,347 (19.2%). Of those accepted, 4,689 enrolled, a [yield rate](/wiki/Yield_(college_admissions) "Yield (college admissions)") (the percentage of accepted students who choose to attend the university) of 45.3%. UNC-Chapel Hill's freshman [retention rate](/wiki/University_student_retention "University student retention") is 96.5%, with 91.9% going on to graduate within six years.[[134]](#cite_note-FallEnrollmentReport-134)[[137]](#cite_note-CP-137)

Of the 60% of enrolled freshmen in 2021 who submitted [ACT](/wiki/ACT_(test) "ACT (test)") scores; the middle 50 percent Composite score was between 29 and 33. Of the 15% of the incoming freshman class who submitted [SAT](/wiki/SAT "SAT") scores; the middle 50 percent Composite scores were 1330–1500.[[134]](#cite_note-FallEnrollmentReport-134) In the 2020–2021 academic year, 20 freshman students were [National Merit Scholars](/wiki/National_Merit_Scholars "National Merit Scholars").[[138]](#cite_note-138) The university is [need-blind](/wiki/Need-blind "Need-blind") for domestic applicants.[[139]](#cite_note-139)

### Honor code

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Honor code")]

The university has a longstanding [honor code](/wiki/Academic_honor_code "Academic honor code") known as the "Instrument of Student Judicial Governance", supplemented by a mostly student-run honor system to resolve issues with students accused of academic and conduct offenses against the university community.[[140]](#cite_note-140)

In 1974, the Judicial Reform Committee created the *Instrument of Student Judicial Governance*, which outlined the current honor code and its means for enforcement.[[141]](#cite_note-The_Daily_Tar_Heel-141) The creation of the instrument and the judicial reform committee was preceded by a list of "Demands by the Black Student Movement" ([BSM](/wiki/Black_Student_Movement "Black Student Movement")) which stated that "[e]ither Black students have full jurisdiction over all offenses committed by Black students or duly elected Black Students from BSM who would represent our interests be on the present Judiciary Courts."[[142]](#cite_note-142) Until 2024, most academic and conduct violations were handled by a single, student-run honor system. Prior to the student-run honor system, the [Dialectic and Philanthropic Societies](/wiki/Dialectic_and_Philanthropic_Societies "Dialectic and Philanthropic Societies"), along with other campus organizations such as the men's council, women's council, and student council supported student concerns.[[143]](#cite_note-143) In 2024, the university transitioned from the student-run honor system to a staff-run "hearing board".[[144]](#cite_note-144)

### Libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Libraries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/The_Davis_Library%2C_UNC_Chapel_Hill.JPG/250px-The_Davis_Library%2C_UNC_Chapel_Hill.JPG)](/wiki/File:The_Davis_Library,_UNC_Chapel_Hill.JPG)

Davis Library

[![A large building with six Corinthian columns on the front with the sides being obscured by bushes.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Louis_Round_Wilson_Library.JPG/250px-Louis_Round_Wilson_Library.JPG)](/wiki/File:Louis_Round_Wilson_Library.JPG)

[Louis Round Wilson Library](/wiki/Louis_Round_Wilson_Library "Louis Round Wilson Library") opened in 1929 and houses special collections.[[145]](#cite_note-145)

UNC-Chapel Hill's library system includes a number of individual libraries housed throughout the campus and holds more than 10 million combined print and electronic volumes.[[146]](#cite_note-146) As of 2025, there were 12 library locations in operation.[[147]](#cite_note-147) UNC-Chapel Hill's [North Carolina Collection](/wiki/North_Carolina_Collection "North Carolina Collection") (NCC) is the largest and most comprehensive collection of holdings about any single state nationwide.[[148]](#cite_note-148) The unparalleled assemblage of literary, visual, and artifactual materials documents four centuries of North Carolina history and culture.[[149]](#cite_note-149) The North Carolina Collection is housed in [Wilson Library](/wiki/Wilson_Library "Wilson Library"), named after [Louis Round Wilson](/wiki/Louis_Round_Wilson "Louis Round Wilson"), along with the [Southern Historical Collection](/wiki/Southern_Historical_Collection "Southern Historical Collection"), the Rare Books Collection, and the [Southern Folklife Collection](/wiki/Southern_Folklife_Collection "Southern Folklife Collection").[[150]](#cite_note-150) The university is home to [ibiblio](/wiki/Ibiblio "Ibiblio"), one of the world's largest collections of freely available information including software, music, literature, art, history, science, politics, and cultural studies.[[151]](#cite_note-151)[[152]](#cite_note-152)

The Davis Library, situated near the Pit, is the main library and the largest academic facility and state-owned building in North Carolina.[[90]](#cite_note-:1-90) It was named after North Carolina philanthropist [Walter Royal Davis](/wiki/Walter_R._Davis "Walter R. Davis") and opened on February 6, 1984. The first book checked out of Davis Library was [George Orwell](/wiki/George_Orwell "George Orwell")'s [*1984*](/wiki/Nineteen_Eighty-Four "Nineteen Eighty-Four").[[153]](#cite_note-153) The R.B. House Undergraduate Library is located between the Pit area and Wilson Library. It is named after Robert B. House, the Chancellor of UNC from 1945 to 1957, and opened in 1968.[[154]](#cite_note-154) In 2001, the R.B. House Undergraduate Library underwent a $9.9 million renovation that modernized the furnishings, equipment, and infrastructure of the building.[[155]](#cite_note-155) Prior to the construction of Davis, Wilson Library was the university's main library, but now Wilson hosts special events and houses special collections, rare books, and temporary exhibits.[[156]](#cite_note-156)

#### *Documenting the American South*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Documenting the American South")]

The library oversees ***Documenting the American South***, a free public access website of "digitized primary materials that offer Southern perspectives on American history and culture." The project began in 1996.[[157]](#cite_note-157) In 2009 the library launched the North Carolina Digital Heritage Center, a statewide digital library, in partnership with other organizations.[[158]](#cite_note-158)

### Scholarships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Scholarships")]

See also: [Morehead-Cain Scholarship](/wiki/Morehead-Cain_Scholarship "Morehead-Cain Scholarship") and [Robertson Scholars Program](/wiki/Robertson_Scholars_Program "Robertson Scholars Program")

For decades, UNC-Chapel Hill has offered an undergraduate merit scholarship known as the [Morehead-Cain Scholarship](/wiki/Morehead-Cain_Scholarship "Morehead-Cain Scholarship"). Recipients receive full tuition, room and board, books, and funds for summer study for four years. Since the inception of the Morehead, 29 alumni of the program have been named [Rhodes Scholars](/wiki/Rhodes_Scholarship "Rhodes Scholarship").[[172]](#cite_note-172) Since 2001, North Carolina has also co-hosted the [Robertson Scholars Leadership Program](/wiki/Robertson_Scholars_Program "Robertson Scholars Program"), a merit scholarship and leadership development program granting recipients full student privileges at both UNC-Chapel Hill and neighboring [Duke University](/wiki/Duke_University "Duke University").[[173]](#cite_note-173) Additionally, the university provides scholarships based on merit and leadership qualities, including the Carolina, Colonel Robinson, Johnston and Pogue Scholars programs.[[174]](#cite_note-174)

In 2003, Chancellor [James Moeser](/wiki/James_Moeser "James Moeser") announced the Carolina Covenant, wherein UNC offers a debt free education to low-income students who are accepted to the university. The program was the first of its kind at a public university and the second overall in the nation (following [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University")). About 80 other universities have since followed suit.[[175]](#cite_note-175)

## Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Athletics")]

Main article: [North Carolina Tar Heels](/wiki/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels "North Carolina Tar Heels")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_logo.svg/250px-North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_logo.svg)

UNC Athletics logo

North Carolina's athletic teams are known as the Tar Heels. They compete as a member of the [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") (NCAA) [Division I](/wiki/Division_I_(NCAA) "Division I (NCAA)") level (Football Bowl Subdivision (FBS) sub-level for football), primarily competing in the [Atlantic Coast Conference](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_Conference "Atlantic Coast Conference") (ACC) for all sports since the 1953–54 season.[[176]](#cite_note-acc-176) Men's sports include baseball, basketball, cross country, fencing, football, golf, lacrosse, soccer, swimming & diving, tennis, track & field and [wrestling](/wiki/College_wrestling "College wrestling"); while women's sports include basketball, cross country, fencing, field hockey, golf, gymnastics, lacrosse, rowing, soccer, softball, swimming and diving, tennis, track & field and volleyball.[[177]](#cite_note-177)

The NCAA refers to UNC-Chapel Hill as the "University of North Carolina" for athletics.[[12]](#cite_note-ncaaschools-12) As of December 2024, the university had won 51 NCAA team championships in eight different sports, tied for 7th all-time.[[178]](#cite_note-NCAA-178) These include twenty two NCAA championships in [women's soccer](/wiki/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_women%27s_soccer "North Carolina Tar Heels women's soccer"), eleven in [women's field hockey](/wiki/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_field_hockey "North Carolina Tar Heels field hockey"), five in [men's lacrosse](/wiki/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_men%27s_lacrosse "North Carolina Tar Heels men's lacrosse"), six in [men's basketball](/wiki/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_men%27s_basketball "North Carolina Tar Heels men's basketball"), one in [women's basketball](/wiki/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_women%27s_basketball "North Carolina Tar Heels women's basketball"), one in women's tennis, three in women's lacrosse, and two in [men's soccer](/wiki/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_men%27s_soccer "North Carolina Tar Heels men's soccer").[[179]](#cite_note-179) Consensus collegiate national athletes of the year from North Carolina include [Rachel Dawson](/wiki/Rachel_Dawson "Rachel Dawson"), [Erin Matson](/wiki/Erin_Matson_(field_hockey) "Erin Matson (field hockey)"), [Katelyn Falgowski](/wiki/Katelyn_Falgowski "Katelyn Falgowski"), [Ryleigh Heck](/wiki/Ryleigh_Heck "Ryleigh Heck"), [Ashley Hoffman](/wiki/Ashley_Hoffman "Ashley Hoffman"), [Leslie Lyness](/wiki/Leslie_Lyness "Leslie Lyness"), [Cindy Werley](/wiki/Cindy_Werley "Cindy Werley") in field hockey; [Phil Ford](/wiki/Phil_Ford_(basketball) "Phil Ford (basketball)"), [Kenny Smith](/wiki/Kenny_Smith "Kenny Smith"), [Sean May](/wiki/Sean_May "Sean May"), [Lennie Rosenbluth](/wiki/Lennie_Rosenbluth "Lennie Rosenbluth"), [Tyler Hansbrough](/wiki/Tyler_Hansbrough "Tyler Hansbrough"), [Jerry Stackhouse](/wiki/Jerry_Stackhouse "Jerry Stackhouse"), [Antawn Jamison](/wiki/Antawn_Jamison "Antawn Jamison"), [James Worthy](/wiki/James_Worthy "James Worthy") and [Michael Jordan](/wiki/Michael_Jordan "Michael Jordan") in men's basketball; and [Mia Hamm](/wiki/Mia_Hamm "Mia Hamm") (twice), [Yael Averbuch](/wiki/Yael_Averbuch "Yael Averbuch"), [Amber Brooks](/wiki/Amber_Brooks "Amber Brooks"), [Crystal Dunn](/wiki/Crystal_Dunn "Crystal Dunn"), [Whitney Engen](/wiki/Whitney_Engen "Whitney Engen"), [Kate Faasse](/wiki/Kate_Faasse "Kate Faasse"), [Lorrie Fair](/wiki/Lorrie_Fair "Lorrie Fair"), [Meredith Florance](/wiki/Meredith_Florance "Meredith Florance"), [April Henrichs](/wiki/April_Henrichs "April Henrichs"), [Debbie Keller](/wiki/Debbie_Keller "Debbie Keller"), [Casey Nogueira](/wiki/Casey_Nogueira "Casey Nogueira"), [Heather O'Reilly](/wiki/Heather_O%27Reilly "Heather O'Reilly"), [Cindy Parlow](/wiki/Cindy_Parlow "Cindy Parlow"), [Catherine Reddick](/wiki/Catherine_Reddick "Catherine Reddick"), [Lindsay Tarpley](/wiki/Lindsay_Tarpley "Lindsay Tarpley"), [Shannon Higgins](/wiki/Shannon_Higgins-Cirovski "Shannon Higgins-Cirovski"), [Kristine Lilly](/wiki/Kristine_Lilly "Kristine Lilly"), and [Tisha Venturini](/wiki/Tisha_Venturini "Tisha Venturini") in women's soccer.[[180]](#cite_note-180)

### Men's basketball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Men's basketball")]

See also: [North Carolina Tar Heels men's basketball](/wiki/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_men%27s_basketball "North Carolina Tar Heels men's basketball")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Dean_Smith_Center_with_game_in_session.JPG/250px-Dean_Smith_Center_with_game_in_session.JPG)](/wiki/File:Dean_Smith_Center_with_game_in_session.JPG)

A UNC Basketball game at the [Dean Smith Center](/wiki/Dean_Smith_Center "Dean Smith Center")

Carolina has enjoyed long success as one of the top college basketball programs in the country. The program claims 7 national championship teams, six [NCAA National Championships](/wiki/NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Championship "NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Championship") and one retroactive championship, for the [1924](/wiki/1923%E2%80%9324_North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_men%27s_basketball_team "1923–24 North Carolina Tar Heels men's basketball team"). This championship was awarded by the [Helms Foundation](/wiki/Helms_Athletic_Foundation "Helms Athletic Foundation") and the [Premo-Porretta Power Poll](/wiki/Premo-Porretta_Power_Poll "Premo-Porretta Power Poll").[[181]](#cite_note-181) UNC has also won eighteen [Atlantic Coast Conference tournament](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_Conference_men%27s_basketball_tournament "Atlantic Coast Conference men's basketball tournament") (ACC) titles,[[182]](#cite_note-grfx.cstv.com-182) and thirty-three [ACC regular season](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_Conference_men%27s_basketball "Atlantic Coast Conference men's basketball") titles.[[183]](#cite_note-UNC-Duke_Postgame_Notes-183)

Under coach [Frank McGuire](/wiki/Frank_McGuire "Frank McGuire"), the Tar Heels won one national championship in 1957. The [1956-57 team](/wiki/1956-57_North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_men%27s_basketball_team "1956-57 North Carolina Tar Heels men's basketball team") went undefeated on their way to the school's first NCAA tournament championship.[[184]](#cite_note-184) McGuire was succeeded by [Dean Smith](/wiki/Dean_Smith "Dean Smith"). After struggling early in his tenure, Smith entrenched the Tar Heels as a basketball powerhouse over his 36 years as head coach. At the time of his retirement, Smith's 879 wins set the record for the most wins of any men's college basketball head coach.[[185]](#cite_note-185) Under Smith, the Tar Heels won two national championships, 13 ACC Tournament championships, and one [NIT Championship](/wiki/National_Invitation_Tournament#Men's_postseason_NIT_champions "National Invitation Tournament"). Smith is also credited with popularizing the [four corners](/wiki/Four_corners_offense "Four corners offense"), which he employed until the introduction of the [shot clock](/wiki/Shot_clock "Shot clock") in college basketball. Smith is also credited with developing "[The Carolina Way](/wiki/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_men%27s_basketball#The_Carolina_Way "North Carolina Tar Heels men's basketball")," epitomized by his motto of "Play hard, play smart, play together," and by other team-oriented practices including "point to the passer," where the player who scores a basket thanks his teammate for the assist.[[186]](#cite_note-186)

In 2003, [Roy Williams](/wiki/Roy_Williams_(basketball_coach) "Roy Williams (basketball coach)"), an assistant under Smith from 1978 to 1988 and the head coach of [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas_Jayhawks_men%27s_basketball "Kansas Jayhawks men's basketball"), returned to his alma mater. In Williams' second season as head coach, the Tar Heels won the 2005 NCAA national championship.[[187]](#cite_note-187) Williams would go on to win two more national titles (2009 and 2017) in his 18 seasons as Tar Heel head coach.[[188]](#cite_note-188)[[189]](#cite_note-189) Williams passed his mentor Smith's 879 win total, finishing his career with 903 wins, 485 of which came in Chapel Hill.[[190]](#cite_note-190)

Williams retired on April 1, 2021, and was replaced by assistant coach [Hubert Davis](/wiki/Hubert_Davis "Hubert Davis").[[191]](#cite_note-191) Davis, who played for Tar Heels from 1988 to 1992 under Smith, also had a lengthy career as an NBA player, and spent several seasons as an analyst for [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") before being hired by Williams as an assistant coach in 2012. He became the first [African American](/wiki/African_American "African American") head coach for UNC men's basketball, and led the team to its NCAA-record 21st final four in the 2021-22 season.[[192]](#cite_note-192)[[193]](#cite_note-193) On March 24, 2026, Carolina fired Davis after five seasons at the helm.[[194]](#cite_note-194)[[195]](#cite_note-195)

[Karen Shelton](/wiki/Karen_Shelton "Karen Shelton") led the Carolina field hockey program for 42 years prior to her retirement following the 2022 season.[[196]](#cite_note-196) She won 10 NCAA national championships and 25 ACC titles, both records for the sport.[[197]](#cite_note-197) Shelton was replaced by former star player [Erin Matson](/wiki/Erin_Matson_(field_hockey) "Erin Matson (field hockey)"), who herself was a member of four of UNC's national championship teams (2018–2020, 2022), and is the only athlete to win the ACC's player of the year award five times.[[198]](#cite_note-198) In Matson's first season in 2023, she led the Tar Heels to their 11th national title, becoming the youngest head coach ever to win a Division I national title in any sport (at age 23).[[199]](#cite_note-199)

### Mascot and nickname

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Mascot and nickname")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/UNC_chapel_hill_ram_statue.jpg/250px-UNC_chapel_hill_ram_statue.jpg)](/wiki/File:UNC_chapel_hill_ram_statue.jpg)

Campus statue of the ram mascot

[![A man dressed as a devil and a ram face each other.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/f/f8/Blue_Devil_and_Rameses.jpg/250px-Blue_Devil_and_Rameses.jpg)](/wiki/File:Blue_Devil_and_Rameses.jpg)

Duke University's "Blue Devil" mascot and UNC's Rameses face off at the 1957 [Victory Bell](/wiki/Victory_Bell_(Duke%E2%80%93North_Carolina) "Victory Bell (Duke–North Carolina)") football game.

The university's teams are nicknamed the "[Tar Heels](/wiki/Tar_Heel "Tar Heel")", in reference to the state's eighteenth-century prominence as a [tar](/wiki/Tar "Tar") and [pitch](/wiki/Pitch_(resin) "Pitch (resin)") producer.[[200]](#cite_note-heel-200) The nickname's cultural relevance, however, has a complex history that includes anecdotal tales from both the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") and the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution").[[200]](#cite_note-heel-200) The mascot is a live [Dorset ram](/wiki/Dorset_Horn "Dorset Horn") named [Rameses](/wiki/Rameses_(mascot) "Rameses (mascot)"), a tradition that dates back to 1924, when the team manager brought a ram to the annual game against [Virginia Military Institute](/wiki/Virginia_Military_Institute "Virginia Military Institute"), inspired by the play of former football player Jack "[The Battering Ram](/wiki/Battering_ram "Battering ram")" Merrit. The kicker rubbed his head for good luck before a game-winning field goal, and the ram stayed.[[201]](#cite_note-201) There is also an [anthropomorphic](/wiki/Anthropomorphic "Anthropomorphic") ram mascot who appears at games.[[202]](#cite_note-202) The modern Rameses is depicted in a sailor's hat, a reference to a [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") flight training program that was attached to the university during World War II.[[203]](#cite_note-203)

### The Carolina Way

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: The Carolina Way")]

Basketball coach [Dean Smith](/wiki/Dean_Smith "Dean Smith") was widely known for his idea of "The Carolina Way", in which he challenged his players to, "Play hard, play smart, play together."[[204]](#cite_note-204) "The Carolina Way" was an idea of excellence in the classroom, as well as on the court. In Coach Smith's book, *The Carolina Way*, former player Scott Williams said, regarding Dean Smith, "Winning was very important at Carolina, and there was much pressure to win, but Coach cared more about our getting a sound education and turning into good citizens than he did about winning."[[205]](#cite_note-205)

The October 22, 2014, release of the Wainstein Report[[206]](#cite_note-206) alleged institutionalized academic fraud that involved over 3,100 students and student athletes, over an 18-year period from 1993 to 2011 that began during the final years of the Dean Smith era, challenged "The Carolina Way" image.[[207]](#cite_note-207) The report alleged that at least 54 players during the Dean Smith era were enrolled in what came to be known as "paper classes". The report noted that the questionable classes began in the spring of 1993, the year of Smith's final championship, so those grades would not have been entered until after the championship game was played.[[208]](#cite_note-208) In response to the allegations of the Wainstein report, the [NCAA](/wiki/NCAA "NCAA") launched their own investigation and on June 5, 2015[[209]](#cite_note-209) the NCAA accused the institution of five major violations including: "two instances of unethical conduct and failure to cooperate" as well as "unethical conduct and extra benefits related to student-athletes' access to and assistance in the paper courses; unethical conduct by the instructor/counselor for providing impermissible academic assistance to student-athletes; and a failure to monitor and lack of institutional control".[[210]](#cite_note-:0-210) In October 2017, the NCAA issued its findings and concluded "that the only violations in this case are the department chair's and the secretary's failure to cooperate".[[210]](#cite_note-:0-210)

### Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

Main articles: [Carolina–Duke rivalry](/wiki/Carolina%E2%80%93Duke_rivalry "Carolina–Duke rivalry"), [North Carolina–NC State rivalry](/wiki/North_Carolina%E2%80%93NC_State_rivalry "North Carolina–NC State rivalry"), and [South's Oldest Rivalry](/wiki/South%27s_Oldest_Rivalry "South's Oldest Rivalry")

The [South's Oldest Rivalry](/wiki/South%27s_Oldest_Rivalry "South's Oldest Rivalry") between North Carolina and its first opponent, the [University of Virginia](/wiki/University_of_Virginia "University of Virginia"), was prominent throughout the first third of the twentieth century.[[211]](#cite_note-211) The 119th meeting in [football](/wiki/College_football "College football") between two of the top public universities in [the east](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States") occurred in October 2014.[[212]](#cite_note-212)

One of the fiercest rivalries is with Durham's [Duke University](/wiki/Duke_University "Duke University"). Located only eight miles from each other, the schools regularly compete in both athletics and academics.[[213]](#cite_note-213) The [Carolina-Duke rivalry](/wiki/Carolina-Duke_rivalry "Carolina-Duke rivalry") is most intense, however, in [basketball](/wiki/College_basketball "College basketball").[[214]](#cite_note-hbo-214) With a combined eleven national championships in men's basketball, both teams have been frequent contenders for the national championship for the past thirty years. The rivalry has been the focus of several books, including Will Blythe's *To Hate Like This Is to Be Happy Forever* and was the focus of the HBO documentary *Battle for Tobacco Road: Duke vs Carolina*.[[215]](#cite_note-215)

Carolina holds an in-state rivalry with fellow [Tobacco Road](/wiki/Tobacco_Road_(rivalry) "Tobacco Road (rivalry)") school, [North Carolina State University](/wiki/North_Carolina_State_University "North Carolina State University"). Since the mid-1970s, however, the Tar Heels have shifted their attention to Duke following a severe decline in NC State's basketball program (and the resurgence of Duke's basketball program) that reached rock bottom during Roy Williams' tenure as evidenced by their 4–36 record against the Tar Heels. The Wolfpack faithful still consider the rivalry the most bitter in the state despite the fact that it's been decades since Tar Heel supporters have acknowledged NC State as a rival. Combined, the two schools hold eight NCAA Championships and 27 ACC Championships in basketball. Students from each school often exchange pranks before basketball and football games.[[216]](#cite_note-216)[[217]](#cite_note-217)

### Rushing Franklin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Rushing Franklin")]

[![A large gathering of people on a street with a bonfire in the right side of the image.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Franklin_Street_Celebration.jpg/250px-Franklin_Street_Celebration.jpg)](/wiki/File:Franklin_Street_Celebration.jpg)

Celebration on [Franklin Street](/wiki/Franklin_Street_(Chapel_Hill) "Franklin Street (Chapel Hill)") after victory over Duke

While students previously held "Beat [Duke](/wiki/Duke_University "Duke University")" parades on [Franklin Street](/wiki/Franklin_Street_(Chapel_Hill) "Franklin Street (Chapel Hill)") before sporting events,[[218]](#cite_note-218) today students and sports fans have been known to spill out of bars and residence halls upon the victory of one of Carolina's sports [teams](/wiki/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels "North Carolina Tar Heels").[[219]](#cite_note-scene_from_franklin-219) In most cases, a Franklin Street "bonfire" celebration is due to a [victory](/wiki/Carolina-Duke_rivalry "Carolina-Duke rivalry") by the [men's basketball team](/wiki/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_men%27s_basketball "North Carolina Tar Heels men's basketball"),[[220]](#cite_note-bonfire-220)[[221]](#cite_note-fire-221) although other Franklin Street celebrations have stemmed from wins by the [women's basketball team](/wiki/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_women%27s_basketball "North Carolina Tar Heels women's basketball") and [women's soccer team](/wiki/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels "North Carolina Tar Heels"). The first known student celebration on Franklin Street came after the [1957 men's basketball team](/wiki/1956%E2%80%9357_North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_men%27s_basketball_team "1956–57 North Carolina Tar Heels men's basketball team") capped their perfect season with a national championship victory over the [Kansas Jayhawks](/wiki/Kansas_Jayhawks "Kansas Jayhawks").[[222]](#cite_note-chapelhillnews1-222) From then on, students have flooded the street after important victories.[[222]](#cite_note-chapelhillnews1-222) After a [Final Four](/wiki/Final_Four "Final Four") victory in [1981](/wiki/1981_NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Tournament "1981 NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Tournament") and the [men's basketball team](/wiki/1981%E2%80%9382_North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_men%27s_basketball_team "1981–82 North Carolina Tar Heels men's basketball team") won the [1982 NCAA Championship](/wiki/1982_NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Tournament "1982 NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Tournament"), Franklin Street was painted blue by the fans who had rushed the street.[[222]](#cite_note-chapelhillnews1-222)

### School colors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: School colors")]

Since the beginning of intercollegiate athletics at UNC in the late nineteenth century, the school's colors have been blue and white.[[223]](#cite_note-223) The colors were chosen years before by the [Dialectic (blue) and Philanthropic (white) Societies](/wiki/Dialectic_and_Philanthropic_Societies "Dialectic and Philanthropic Societies"), the oldest student organization at the university. The school had required participation in one of the clubs, and traditionally the "Di"s were from the western part of North Carolina while the "Phi"s were from the eastern part of the state.[[224]](#cite_note-Carolina_Review,_vol._XIII,_no._6-224)

[![A bunch of people standing with cap and gowns while two people stand on a grass field.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Commencement_Ceremony_at_Kenan_Memorial_Stadium.jpg/250px-Commencement_Ceremony_at_Kenan_Memorial_Stadium.jpg)](/wiki/File:Commencement_Ceremony_at_Kenan_Memorial_Stadium.jpg)

The 2007 commencement ceremony in [Kenan Stadium](/wiki/Kenan_Stadium "Kenan Stadium")

Society members would wear a blue or white ribbon at university functions, and blue or white ribbons were attached to their diplomas at graduation.[[224]](#cite_note-Carolina_Review,_vol._XIII,_no._6-224) On public occasions, both groups were equally represented, and eventually both colors were used by processional leaders to signify the unity of both groups as part of the university.[[225]](#cite_note-225) When football became a popular collegiate sport in the 1880s, the [Carolina football](/wiki/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_football "North Carolina Tar Heels football") team adopted the light blue and white of the Di-Phi Societies as the school colors.[[226]](#cite_note-226)

### School songs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: School songs")]

Notable among a number of songs commonly played and sung at various events such as [commencement](/wiki/Graduation "Graduation"), [convocation](/wiki/Convocation "Convocation"), and athletic games are the university [fight songs](/wiki/Fight_song "Fight song") "[I'm a Tar Heel Born](/wiki/I%27m_a_Tar_Heel_Born "I'm a Tar Heel Born")" and "[Here Comes Carolina](/wiki/Here_Comes_Carolina "Here Comes Carolina")".[[227]](#cite_note-songs-227) The fight songs are often played by the bell tower near the center of campus, as well as after major victories.[[227]](#cite_note-songs-227) "I'm a Tar Heel Born" originated in the late 1920s as a tag to the school's alma mater, "[Hark The Sound](/wiki/Hark_The_Sound "Hark The Sound")".[[227]](#cite_note-songs-227)
