### Organizations and activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Organizations and activities")]

Most student organizations at UNC-Chapel Hill are officially recognized and provided with assistance by the Carolina Union, an administrative unit of the university.[[231]](#cite_note-234) Funding is derived from the [student government](/wiki/Student_government "Student government") student activity fee, which is allocated at the discretion of the Undergraduate Senate (UGS) or the Graduate and Professional Student Government Senate (GPSG Senate).[[232]](#cite_note-235)

The largest student fundraiser, the UNC Dance Marathon, involves thousands of students, faculty, and community members in raising funds for the [North Carolina Children's Hospital](/wiki/UNC_Health_Care "UNC Health Care"). The organization conducts fundraising and volunteer activities throughout the year and, as of 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit), had donated $1.4 million since its inception in 1999.[[233]](#cite_note-236)

The student-run newspaper *[The Daily Tar Heel](/wiki/The_Daily_Tar_Heel "The Daily Tar Heel")* received the 2004–2005 [National Pacemaker Award](/wiki/National_Pacemaker_Awards "National Pacemaker Awards") from the [Associated Collegiate Press](/wiki/Associated_Collegiate_Press "Associated Collegiate Press").[[234]](#cite_note-237) Founded in 1977, [WXYC](/wiki/WXYC "WXYC") 89.3 FM is UNC-Chapel Hill's student radio station that broadcasts 24 hours a day, 365 days a year. Programming is left up to student DJs. WXYC typically plays little heard music from a wide range of genres and eras. On November 7, 1994, WXYC became the first radio station in the world to broadcast its signal over the internet.[[235]](#cite_note-238)[[236]](#cite_note-239) A student-run television station, STV, airs on the campus cable and throughout the Chapel Hill [Spectrum](/wiki/Spectrum_(TV_service) "Spectrum (TV service)") system.[[237]](#cite_note-240) Founded in 1948 as successor to the *Carolina Magazine*,[[238]](#cite_note-241) the *Carolina Quarterly*, edited by graduate students, has published the works of numerous authors, including [Wendell Berry](/wiki/Wendell_Berry "Wendell Berry"), [Raymond Carver](/wiki/Raymond_Carver "Raymond Carver"), [Don DeLillo](/wiki/Don_DeLillo "Don DeLillo"), [Annie Dillard](/wiki/Annie_Dillard "Annie Dillard"), [Joyce Carol Oates](/wiki/Joyce_Carol_Oates "Joyce Carol Oates"), and [John Edgar Wideman](/wiki/John_Edgar_Wideman "John Edgar Wideman"). Works appearing in the *Quarterly* have been anthologized in *[Best American Short Stories](/wiki/Best_American_Short_Stories "Best American Short Stories")* and *[New Stories from the South](/wiki/New_Stories_from_the_South "New Stories from the South")*[[239]](#cite_note-242) and have won the [Pushcart](/wiki/Pushcart_Prize "Pushcart Prize") and [O. Henry Prizes](/wiki/O._Henry_Prize "O. Henry Prize").[[240]](#cite_note-243)

The *Clef Hangers* (also known as the *Clefs*) are the university's oldest [a cappella](/wiki/Collegiate_a_cappella "Collegiate a cappella") group, founded by Barry Saunders in 1977.[[241]](#cite_note-244)[[242]](#cite_note-RARB-245) The group has since won several Contemporary A Cappella Recording Awards (CARAs), including Best Soloist in the song *Easy*, featured on the 2003 album *Breeze*. They have won two more CARAs for Best Male Collegiate Songs for *My Love on Time Out* (2008),[[243]](#cite_note-246) and for *Ain't Nothing Wrong on Twist* (2009).[[244]](#cite_note-247) Members have included [Brendan James](/wiki/Brendan_James "Brendan James"), who graduated in 2002,[[245]](#cite_note-248) and [Anoop Desai](/wiki/Anoop_Desai "Anoop Desai"), who graduated in 2008.[[242]](#cite_note-RARB-245)

The Residence Hall Association, the school's third-largest student-run organization, is the representative organization for students living in residence halls. Its activities include social, educational, and philanthropic programs for residents; recognizing outstanding residents and members; and helping residents develop into successful leaders.[[246]](#cite_note-249) RHA is the affiliated to the [National Association of College and University Residence Halls](/wiki/National_Association_of_College_and_University_Residence_Halls "National Association of College and University Residence Halls").[[247]](#cite_note-250)

[![A large gathering of people in a room.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/UNC_library_flash_mob_rave.jpg/250px-UNC_library_flash_mob_rave.jpg)](/wiki/File:UNC_library_flash_mob_rave.jpg)

At the end of each semester, students organize a [flash mob](/wiki/Flash_mob "Flash mob") dance party in the library.[[248]](#cite_note-251)

UNC also has a biannual [naked run](/wiki/Streaking "Streaking"). On the day before final exams, students gather on the 8th floor of the Davis Library, strip naked, and streak down through the floors and back up. This is done to deal with exam stress and to increase body positivity.[[249]](#cite_note-252)

The athletic teams at the university are supported by [The Marching Tar Heels](/wiki/The_Marching_Tar_Heels "The Marching Tar Heels"), the university's [marching band](/wiki/Marching_band "Marching band"). The entire 275-member volunteer band is present at every home football game, and smaller [pep bands](/wiki/Pep_band "Pep band") play at all home basketball games. Each member of the band is also required to play in at least one of five pep bands that play at athletic events of the 26 other sports.[[250]](#cite_note-253)

UNC-Chapel Hill has a [regional theater](/wiki/Regional_theatre_in_the_United_States "Regional theatre in the United States") company in residence, the [Playmakers Repertory Company](/wiki/Dramatic_and_performing_arts_at_the_University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill#PlayMakers_Repertory_Company "Dramatic and performing arts at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill"),[[251]](#cite_note-254) and hosts regular dance, drama, and music performances on campus.[[252]](#cite_note-255) The school has an outdoor stone amphitheatre known as Forest Theatre used for weddings and drama productions.[[253]](#cite_note-256) Forest Theatre is dedicated to Professor Frederick Koch, the founder of the [Carolina Playmakers](/wiki/PlayMakers_Repertory_Company#History_of_the_Carolina_Playmakers "PlayMakers Repertory Company") and the father American folk drama.[[254]](#cite_note-257)

Many [fraternities and sororities](/wiki/Fraternities_and_sororities_in_North_America "Fraternities and sororities in North America") on campus belong to the [National Panhellenic Conference](/wiki/National_Panhellenic_Conference "National Panhellenic Conference") (NPC), Interfraternity Council (IFC), Greek Alliance Council, and [National Pan-Hellenic Council](/wiki/National_Pan-Hellenic_Council "National Pan-Hellenic Council") (NPHC). As of spring 2023, sixteen percent of undergraduates were in fraternities or sororities (3131 out of 17,160 total).[[255]](#cite_note-258) UNC-Chapel Hill also offers professional and service fraternities that do not have houses but are still recognized by the school. Some of the campus honor societies include: the Order of the Golden Fleece, the Order of the Grail-Valkyries, the Order of the Old Well, the Order of the Bell Tower, and the Frank Porter Graham Honor Society.[[256]](#cite_note-259)

### Student government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Student government")]

The student government at UNC–Chapel Hill is split into undergraduate student government and graduate and professional student government.[[257]](#cite_note-260) The undergraduate student government consists of an executive branch headed by the student body president[[258]](#cite_note-261) and a legislative branch, the undergraduate student senate.[[259]](#cite_note-262) The graduate and professional student government similarly consists of an executive (with its own president) and a legislative senate.[[260]](#cite_note-263) There is also a joint governance council that approves legislation affecting both undergraduate and graduate and professional students and advises the undergraduate and graduate and professional student governments.[[261]](#cite_note-264) The honor system is similarly split into two branches covering undergraduate students and graduate and professional students.[[262]](#cite_note-265) The Student Supreme Court, the other part of the judicial branch, consists of four associate justices and a chief justice, which are appointed by the student body president and confirmed by a two thirds vote of the senate for their part of the student body.[[263]](#cite_note-266)

### Dining

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Dining")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/2008-07-21_Lenoir_Hall.jpg/250px-2008-07-21_Lenoir_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:2008-07-21_Lenoir_Hall.jpg)

Lenoir Hall

Lenoir Dining Hall was completed in 1939 using funds from the [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal") era [Public Works Administration](/wiki/Public_Works_Administration "Public Works Administration"), and opened for service to students when they returned from Christmas holidays in January 1940. The building was named for [General William Lenoir](/wiki/William_Lenoir_(general) "William Lenoir (general)"), the first chairman of the Board of Trustees of the university in 1790. Since its inception, Lenoir Dining Hall has remained the flagship of Carolina Dining Services and the center of dining on campus. It has been renovated twice, in 1984 and 2011, to improve seating and ease mealtime rushes.[[264]](#cite_note-267)

Chase Hall was originally built in 1965 to offer South Campus dining options and honor former UNC President Harry Woodburn Chase, who served from 1919 to 1930. In 2005, the building was torn down to make way for the Student and Academic Services buildings, and was rebuilt north of the original location as the Rams Head Center (with the inner dining hall officially titled Chase Dining Hall). Due to students nicknaming the dining hall Rams Head, the university officially reinstated Chase Hall as the building name in March 2017. It includes the Chase Dining Hall, the Rams Head Market, and a conference room called the "Blue Zone".[[265]](#cite_note-268) Chase Dining Hall seats 1,300 people and has a capacity for serving 10,000 meals per day.[[266]](#cite_note-269) It continues to offer more food service options to the students living on south campus, and features extended hours including the 9 pm – 12 am period referred to as "Late Night".[[267]](#cite_note-270)

### Housing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Housing")]

Main article: [University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill student housing](/wiki/University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill_student_housing "University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill student housing")

[![beige, brick building with many windows on the right side of the building.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Old_East.jpg/250px-Old_East.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_East.jpg)

[Old East Residence Hall](/wiki/Old_East "Old East"), built in 1793

On campus, the Department of Housing and Residential Education manages thirty-two residence halls, grouped into thirteen communities. These communities range from Olde Campus Upper Quad Community which includes [Old East Residence Hall](/wiki/Old_East "Old East"), the oldest building of the university, to modern communities such as Manning West, completed in 2002.[[268]](#cite_note-271)[[269]](#cite_note-272) First year students are required to live in one of the eight "First Year Experience" residence halls, most of which are located on South Campus.[[270]](#cite_note-273) In addition to residence halls, the university oversees an additional eight apartment complexes organized into three communities, Ram Village, Odum Village, and Baity Hill Student Family Housing. Along with themed housing focusing on foreign languages and substance-free living, there are also "living-learning communities" which have been formed for specific social, gender-related, or academic needs.[[271]](#cite_note-274) An example is UNITAS, sponsored by the Department of Anthropology, where residents are assigned roommates on the basis of cultural or racial differences rather than similarities.[[272]](#cite_note-275) Three apartment complexes offer housing for families, graduate students, and some upperclassmen.[[273]](#cite_note-276) Along with the rest of campus, all residence halls, apartments, and their surrounding grounds are [smoke-free](/wiki/Smoking_ban "Smoking ban").[[274]](#cite_note-277) As of 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill&action=edit), 46% of all undergraduates live in university-provided housing.[[275]](#cite_note-278)
